metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Jira-exclusion guard: `reconcile_check.py` has no `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` map — the only map is in `outbound_fields.py` (re-exported via `outbound_differ.py`). Update the plan to cite only `outbound_fields.py` (or confirm whether `reconcile_check.py` needs its own exclusion guard added).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'leave BOTH `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` maps (`outbound_fields.py`, `reconcile_check.py`) untouched so any leak surfaces' — but `reconcile_check.py` contains NO `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` map. The only map is in `outbound_fields.py`, re-exported by `outbound_differ.py`. Citing `reconcile_check.py` as a second map site is a false premise; a reader implementing this step would look for a non-existent map and be confused.
findings.location: Section A (Jira exclusion) — 'leave BOTH `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` maps (`outbound_fields.py`, `reconcile_check.py`) untouched'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'leave BOTH `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` maps (`outbound_fields.py`, `reconcile_check.py`) untouched' with 'leave the single `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` map in `outbound_fields.py` (re-exported via `outbound_differ.py`) untouched so any `code_review` leak past `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES` surfaces as an unmapped type rather than silently syncing'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_commands/transition.py` and `_commands/txn.py` to the scope list and update their hardcoded `"session_log"` lifecycle-exemption guards to cover `code_review` (e.g., check against `_NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES` or an explicit tuple).
findings.finding: _commands/transition.py (line 215) and _commands/txn.py (lines 171, 380) contain hardcoded `ticket_type == 'session_log'` lifecycle-exemption guards for transition and claim. Neither file appears in the plan's scope list. Without updating them, a `code_review` ticket can be transitioned or claimed (both are defined behaviors since `code_review` IS a valid type in `composer._TYPES`), silently bypassing the lifecycle-exempt contract the plan intends.
findings.location: Section A (guard sites list) / Scope / Files section — _commands/transition.py and _commands/txn.py absent from scope
findings.suggested_fix: In both files, change `ticket_type == 'session_log'` to `ticket_type in ('session_log', 'code_review')` (or a shared constant); add both files to the Scope / Files section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct Section C's emit description: the voter should call `code_review.sidecar.emit(verdict_dict, target_ticket=artifact_id, repo_root=...)` directly (using the verdict already returned by `adapter.code_review_decision`), NOT re-invoke `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` with a target_ticket.
findings.finding: The plan's Section C states the voter should call `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)` to emit the sidecar, but the voter never calls `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` directly — it calls `adapter.code_review_decision` (voter.py:430–447), which internally calls `produce_code_review_verdict` without a `target_ticket`. The verdict dict returned to the voter does not have a path back to `produce_code_review_verdict`; the voter would need to call `code_review.sidecar.emit(verdict, target_ticket=artifact_id)` directly, not re-invoke `produce_code_review_verdict`.
findings.location: Section C — 'reuse the existing gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the Section C sentence starting 'reuse the existing gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(...)' with: 'call `rebar.llm.code_review.sidecar.emit(verdict, target_ticket=artifact_id, repo_root=repo_root)` directly, augmenting the payload with `change_fingerprint` and per-finding `norm_id` before the emit call (or inside `sidecar.build_payload`).' Also verify that the verdict dict returned by adapter.code_review_decision carries enough data for the sidecar payload (it is an adapter-translated dict, not the raw produce_code_review_verdict output).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_io/export_ndjson.py` to the scope list and update the session_log-only export exclusion to also exclude `code_review` by default (or extend `include_session_logs` to a more general `include_artifact_types` flag).
findings.finding: `_io/export_ndjson.py` line 103 has a hardcoded `if ttype == 'session_log' and not include_session_logs: continue` that excludes only `session_log` from default NDJSON exports. This file is not in the plan's scope list. `code_review` artifacts (potentially with large verdict sidecar payloads) will be included in all default exports, unlike `session_log`.
findings.location: Scope / Files section — _io/export_ndjson.py absent

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `code_review` to the export exclusion guard in `src/rebar/_io/export_ndjson.py` (parallel to the `session_log` exclusion at line 103) and add a corresponding `include_code_review_artifacts` (or broaden the existing `include_session_logs` param) to `export_tickets` / `__init__.py:822` so `code_review` artifacts are excluded from default NDJSON exports, mirroring the `session_log` prior-art pattern the plan commits to replicating.
findings.finding: The plan commits to mirroring `session_log`'s exemptions at EVERY guard site, but omits `export_ndjson.py`'s existing hardcoded `if ttype == 'session_log' and not include_session_logs: continue` guard (line 103) and the matching `include_session_logs` parameter on `export_tickets` / `__init__.py:822`. Without adding a parallel exclusion for `code_review`, artifacts are exported by default in NDJSON dumps — inconsistent with the plan's stated goal of fully mirroring `session_log` behavior and likely undesirable for a bulk calibration corpus.
findings.location: Section A ('mirror `session_log` at EVERY guard site') / `src/rebar/_io/export_ndjson.py:103` / `src/rebar/__init__.py:822`
findings.suggested_fix: In `export_ndjson.py`, change the existing guard from `if ttype == 'session_log' and not include_session_logs` to `if ttype in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES and not include_artifact_logs` (or a similarly named boolean), and thread the parameter through `export_tickets` and the `__init__.py` facade. List `export_ndjson.py` in Section A's guard-site inventory and add it to the Scope/files list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the AC4 credential-helper grep to anchor on the actual git-config invocation, e.g. `grep -qE 'git config.*(--local|local).*credential\.' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`, so the command only passes when the per-URL helper line is present.
findings.finding: AC4's grep for the Dockerfile credential helper — `grep -q 'credential' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` — is a substring false-positive (VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT #1): the word 'credential' appears in any comment, ENV var name, or unrelated git config line, so the command passes even if the per-URL credential helper is absent or misconfigured.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 proving command: `grep -q 'credential' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -q 'credential' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` with `grep -qE 'git config[[:space:]].*credential\.' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` to require the actual git config call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC3's test description to require asserting the exact log format (e.g. via `caplog` / `assertLogs` checking that the WARNING message matches the specified format string), so the proving command actually exercises the format guarantee.
findings.finding: Section C specifies an exact WARNING log format (`logger.warning("code_review artifact %s: unresolved rebar-ticket trailer %r (change %s/%s)", ...)`) and an INFO summary format, but AC3's test spec only requires 'logged, not raised' — the proving command (`pytest tests/unit/test_code_review_artifact.py -v`) does not assert the specific format, so the format claim is unverified.
findings.location: Section C (LOG FORMAT paragraph) vs Acceptance Criteria — AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'the WARNING message must match `r"code_review artifact .+: unresolved rebar-ticket trailer .+ \(change .+/.+\)"`; assert via `caplog.records` or `assertLogs` in the unit test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the rename vs. keep-name contradiction: remove 'rename to an artifact-type SET' from Section A and replace with 'broaden the predicate to `ticket_type not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES`; the parameter name `exclude_session_logs` is retained for back-compat (no caller migration needed)'.
findings.finding: Section A instructs to RENAME `exclude_session_logs` to 'an artifact-type SET so every downstream guard inherits it', but Implementation precision contradicts this by saying the name 'KEEPS its name (back-compat — NO caller migration) but its predicate broadens'. An implementer reading both sections will receive conflicting instructions.
findings.location: Section A ('rename to an artifact-type SET') vs. Implementation precision ('KEEPS its name (back-compat — NO caller migration)')
findings.suggested_fix: In Section A, change 'rename to an artifact-type SET so every downstream guard inherits it' to 'introduce a module-level constant `_NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES = {"session_log", "code_review"}` and broaden the existing boolean predicate body to `ticket_type not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES`; the `exclude_session_logs` parameter name is RETAINED (no caller migration)'. Delete the word 'rename' entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the trailer-resolution scope: the `commit_message` in voter.py scope at the emit point is from a single `gc.get_commit` call (one commit); if multi-commit union is truly required, spec the additional Gerrit API call(s) needed to enumerate all commits in the change, or explicitly narrow the spec to 'the patchset's merge commit message only'.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `commit_message` (already in scope after `store.record_vote` in `voter.py`) is sufficient for trailer resolution because `extract_ticket_refs(commit_message)` can be called on it. However the plan also says 'over ALL commits in the change (parse each commit's message, union the refs)'. The actual `commit_message` in scope at voter.py:368 is the SINGLE commit fetched via `gc.get_commit(change_id, revision)` — it is one commit, not all commits in a multi-commit change. Achieving the 'union the refs' across all commits would require a new traversal (e.g. iterating the commit's parents or a separate commits-list API call), which is new code not currently in scope.
findings.location: Section C — 'TRAILER RESOLUTION: `verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(commit_message)` over ALL commits in the change (parse each commit's message, union the refs)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify (or implement) how ALL commit messages in a Gerrit change are fetched for trailer extraction: either (a) use `gc.get_mergelist(change_id, revision)` commit messages for merge changes + the existing `commit_message` for non-merge (which IS the single commit in a squashed change), or (b) add a `get_change_commits()` GerritClient method, or (c) explicitly scope trailer extraction to the patchset HEAD commit only and update the AC to match.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'extract_ticket_refs(commit_message) over ALL commits in the change (parse each commit's message, union the refs)' but the existing GerritClient only fetches the single top-commit message via get_commit(change_id, revision). For a Gerrit change with multiple underlying commits (non-merge, squash=off), only one commit message is available in voter.py's scope at emit time. No get_all_commits() or equivalent iteration over a change's commit list is wired in the voter. This means multi-commit changes will silently under-resolve trailers.
findings.location: Section C — TRAILER RESOLUTION; voter.py review_and_vote implementation scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify Section C's 'ALL commits' scope: either (a) narrow the spec to 'the single commit message already captured in `commit_message`' (sufficient for typical Gerrit single-commit changes and avoids new REST calls), or (b) explicitly name the additional Gerrit REST surface needed (`GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/` + per-revision `get_commit`) and add those methods to `GerritClient` in the scope/files list.
findings.finding: Section C says trailer resolution runs `verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(commit_message)` over 'ALL commits in the change (parse each commit's message, union the refs)'. However, `GerritClient` only exposes `get_commit(change_id, revision)` for the single current revision — there is no existing method to enumerate all commit messages for a multi-patchset or multi-commit change. The voter.py already captures `commit_message` for the single commit. For a Gerrit change that spans multiple commits (unusual but valid), trailers in earlier commits would be silently missed.
findings.location: Section C, step 3: 'TRAILER RESOLUTION: verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(commit_message) over ALL commits in the change (parse each commit's message, union the refs)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback note asserting the post-revert schema state: either (a) the `code_review` enum value is KEPT in the schema on a code-only rollback (records remain valid) and only removed after all persisted `code_review` tickets are archived/deleted, or (b) the validator is confirmed to soft-skip unknown `ticket_type` values so orphaned records cause no errors — whichever is true must be stated explicitly, with a DATA LOSS RISK note if (b) is not confirmed.
findings.finding: The plan never asserts the schema/store state AFTER a rollback: if the `code_review` enum value is removed from `common.schema.json` and `types.py` as part of a revert, any `code_review`-typed records already persisted to the `tickets` branch become unrecognized by the validator, potentially causing parse/validation errors on `show`, `search`, or any ticket-load path that enforces `ticket_type` membership.
findings.location: Section A ('The `code_review` ticket type') and AC line 1; also 'Implementation precision' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback note' to Section A: 'On revert, do NOT remove `code_review` from `common.schema.json` until all persisted `code_review` tickets on the `tickets` branch are deleted or re-typed; removing the enum while records of that type exist will cause validation errors on ticket load. Compensating-forward approach: mark orphaned records as `type: session_log` via a one-shot migration script before removing the enum.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare that `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` MUST be a **fine-grained PAT** scoped to the single `navapbc/rebar` repository with only `Contents: read and write` permission (not a classic `repo` OAuth token), and add a token-expiry / rotation policy to the SSM slot documentation in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`.
findings.finding: The plan does not declare the permission scope (fine-grained vs. classic OAuth, repo-scoped vs. account-wide) of the `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` GitHub PAT that crosses the public-internet trust boundary to push to the `tickets` branch. A classic `repo`-scoped PAT grants write access to every repository the RebarBotNava account can access, far exceeding the minimum required (push to one branch of one repo). The plan mentions 'contents:write PAT' but does not require a fine-grained PAT restricted to the single target repository.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — 'SSM slot: add the leaf `reviewbot-tickets-pat` … (a SecureString the operator populates with the RebarBotNava contents:write PAT)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, update the comment for the new `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` entry to read: 'Fine-grained PAT scoped to navapbc/rebar, Contents: read+write only; rotate annually. Do NOT use a classic repo-scoped OAuth token.' Mirror this requirement in the fetch-secrets.sh comment block and the Dockerfile startup note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_commands/gates.py` to the scope list and update its `('bug', 'session_log')` exemption tuple to include `'code_review'`.
findings.finding: _commands/gates.py line 119 contains `if ticket_type in ('bug', 'session_log'): return None` — the plan-review start-work gate exemption. This file is not in the plan's scope list. Without updating it, a `code_review` artifact that somehow has an open status would NOT be exempt from the plan-review gate on claim, causing a spurious LLM call or gate block.
findings.location: Section A (gate exemption guard sites) / Scope / Files section — _commands/gates.py absent

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default startup behavior explicitly: e.g. 'if the worktree directory already exists, skip `git clone` and run `git fetch origin tickets && git reset --hard origin/tickets` to refresh state before the first review of the session; otherwise clone fresh.'
findings.finding: The Dockerfile startup behavior for the persistent tickets worktree is underspecified: the plan says 'on startup materialize a PERSISTENT tickets worktree once (clone of the tickets branch)' but does not state the behavior when the volume already contains a worktree (container restart / redeploy), nor whether a fetch/pull is performed to avoid stale-state push failures — leaving the executor to choose.
findings.location: Section D — 'on startup materialize a PERSISTENT tickets worktree once (clone of the tickets branch)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section D: 'Startup script: `if [ ! -d "$WORKTREE/.git" ]; then git clone --branch tickets $REMOTE $WORKTREE; else git -C $WORKTREE fetch origin tickets && git -C $WORKTREE reset --hard origin/tickets; fi` — runs idempotently on every container start.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the Dockerfile startup worktree-init script checks for an existing worktree before cloning (e.g. `[ -d $WORKTREE_DIR/.git ] || git worktree add …`) so the step is idempotent across container restarts and auto-deploy rebuilds.
findings.finding: The Dockerfile one-time tickets-worktree materialization has no stated idempotency guard for container restarts. The plan says 'on startup materialize a PERSISTENT tickets worktree once (clone of the `tickets` branch)' but does not specify what happens when the container restarts with the worktree volume already populated — whether the init script checks for the existing worktree before re-cloning (which would corrupt or duplicate it).
findings.location: Section D: 'on startup materialize a PERSISTENT tickets worktree once (clone of the `tickets` branch)'; AC item 4 ('a persistent tickets-worktree volume in docker-compose.yml … one-time tickets-worktree materialization in Dockerfile.reviewbot')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Dockerfile CMD or an ENTRYPOINT init script: `[ -d "${TICKETS_WORKTREE}/.git" ] || git clone --branch tickets <origin> "${TICKETS_WORKTREE}"` — skip the clone if the worktree is already present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the tickets-worktree volume explicitly in the plan (e.g. `reviewbot_tickets_worktree`), declare it `external: true` in `docker-compose.yml`'s `volumes:` block (parallel to `gerrit_reviewbot`), add its creation to `compose-up.sh`, and extend the AC proving command to verify the volume entry.
findings.finding: The plan specifies adding a persistent tickets-worktree volume to `docker-compose.yml` but never names the volume, and the AC proving command does not verify its presence in the `volumes:` block. Without a named external volume (parallel to `gerrit_reviewbot`), the worktree directory would not survive `docker compose down -v` or would be an anonymous volume subject to removal.
findings.location: Section D: 'add a persistent tickets-worktree volume in infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'; AC item 4 (proving command only greps for `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH`, not for the volume declaration)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to docker-compose.yml volumes block: `reviewbot_tickets_worktree:\n  external: true` and add the corresponding `docker volume create --driver local --opt type=none --opt o=bind --opt device=/var/gerrit/site/tickets-worktree reviewbot_tickets_worktree` in compose-up.sh.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact augmentation path for `change_fingerprint` and per-finding `norm_id` into the `code_review_result_v1` sidecar payload: name whether `build_payload` gains new parameters, or the `verdict` dict is pre-enriched in `voter.py` before calling `emit`, and update the `Scope / files` list accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan says to 'augment' `produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)` → `code_review.sidecar.emit` with `change_fingerprint` and per-finding `norm_id`, but the existing `code_review/sidecar.py:build_payload` signature is `(verdict, *, target_ticket)` and neither `change_fingerprint` nor `norm_id` appear in it or in `produce_code_review_verdict`'s signature. The plan never specifies whether the augmentation means: (a) adding `change_fingerprint` / `norm_id` as new parameters to `build_payload`; (b) injecting them into the `verdict` dict before calling `emit`; or (c) adding a new wrapper. This is an undefined processing protocol co-located with a schema (`code_review_result_v1`) that an executor must produce.
findings.location: Section C, step 2 — 'Emit the verdict sidecar … augmented with `change_fingerprint` and per-finding `norm_id`'; `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add (a) at least one retry with backoff on the git push (e.g., up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff for transient failures), (b) a logged ERROR (not just WARNING) with a distinct message on permanent push failure so it is alertable, and (c) a worktree-hygiene step (e.g., `git reset --hard origin/tickets` on startup or after N consecutive failures) to prevent unbounded commit accumulation.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retry, backoff, or recovery strategy for failures in the git push to the tickets branch, and no metric or alert for persistent push failures. A transient network error, an expired PAT, or a merge conflict will cause the push to fail silently (only caught-and-logged); over time unpushed commits accumulate in the worktree with no bound, no cleanup path, and no operator signal that the corpus is stale.
findings.location: Section D — 'All emit/link/push failures are caught and logged; they NEVER fail the review vote'; Section C — emit/link/push failure handling.
findings.suggested_fix: In `voter.py`'s emit path, wrap the push in a helper that retries up to 3 times with 1s/2s/4s backoff, logs `logger.error("code_review push failed permanently for artifact %s after %d attempts", artifact_id, attempts)` on exhaustion, and increments a module-level `PUSH_FAILURE_COUNT` counter inspectable by a health-check endpoint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability probe entry (in `infra/scripts/observability.sh`) that counts WARNING-level artifact-emit/push failures from the reviewbot journald, publishes a `rebar/host:code_review_emit_errors` metric, and add a corresponding CloudWatch alarm in a new `infra/terraform/monitoring_<story>.tf` wired to the shared SNS alerts topic — mirroring the pattern used by `monitoring_s4b.tf`/`monitoring_88ab.tf`.
findings.finding: No CloudWatch alarm or observability probe entry is specified for artifact-emit/push failures. The plan explicitly makes emit/link/push failures non-fatal (caught, logged, vote unaffected), but the WARNING-level log lines they produce are not wired into any existing alarm (the voter_errors alarm watches VOTER_ERROR markers, not artifact-emit failures). Silent push failures would accumulate undetected, defeating the corpus-calibration purpose of the feature.
findings.location: Section D (Infra); Section C (emit path failure handling); AC item 3 ('logged at WARNING … NON-FATAL')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Dockerfile build-time or container-startup self-test step (e.g., `git credential fill` dry-run against the tickets remote URL, or a `git ls-remote` with the PAT) that fails fast and loudly if the per-URL credential helper is mis-scoped, so the deploy canary has a binary pass/fail signal rather than silent first-push failure.
findings.finding: The git credential helper wiring (per-URL, not global, in a persistent worktree inside a container) is a novel and environment-sensitive integration path that is explicitly deferred to a deploy-step canary with no in-repo probe or smoke test. The plan acknowledges it is 'un-verifiable in-session', but there is no spike, shell-script fixture, or CI check that validates the credential helper configuration syntax and scoping are correct before the first live deploy.
findings.location: Section D — 'git config --local credential.<tickets-url>.helper … PER-URL (not global)'; AC item 4 — 'The live on-box PAT push is a DEPLOY-STEP canary (needs SSM + a running container) and is explicitly OUT of in-session verification.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_CAPTURE` env-var flag (default `off` initially, flip to `on` after canary confirms correctness) so artifact emit can be disabled instantly via SSM/compose without a container redeploy.
findings.finding: No feature flag or kill switch exists for the artifact-emit path. Once the updated container is deployed, every review immediately triggers artifact creation and git push on 100% of traffic with no way to disable emit short of a full container redeploy.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) and Section C (reviewbot emit), AC bullet 4
findings.suggested_fix: In voter.py, gate the artifact-emit block with `if os.getenv('REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_CAPTURE', 'off').lower() == 'on':`. Add the variable to docker-compose.yml (initially `off`), fetch-secrets.sh, and the AC grep check. This gives a trivial, testable kill switch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the required deploy ordering (populate SSM secret BEFORE container start), add a startup health check or retry loop for the worktree clone that fails fast with a clear error if `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` is empty, and document the recovery procedure (volume wipe + container restart) if the worktree enters a corrupted state.
findings.finding: Deploy ordering between SSM secret population and container startup is under-specified: if the container starts (or restarts) before the operator has populated `reviewbot-tickets-pat` in SSM, the credential helper will echo an empty password, causing all git pushes to fail silently (non-fatal), but also causing the persistent worktree's one-time clone to fail at startup — leaving the worktree in an absent or half-initialized state for the container's lifetime.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — SSM slot + Dockerfile one-time worktree materialization

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the single-worker serialization as the concurrency contract for idempotency, and add a unique-constraint or store-level deduplication guard (e.g., check for existing artifact inside the same serialized task, not via a separate list call) so the invariant is enforced structurally rather than by deployment assumption.
findings.finding: The idempotency check (list_tickets → match title → create-if-absent) is a check-then-act (TOCTOU) pattern. The plan relies implicitly on the single background worker serializing all reviews to prevent a race, but this serialization guarantee is never stated as the concurrency contract. If the background worker is ever scaled out or the serialization assumption breaks, concurrent reviews of the same (change-id, revision) will both pass the lookup, both create, and produce duplicate artifacts.
findings.location: Section C, Implementation precision — idempotency lookup; Section D — 'single background worker serializes reviews'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the O(n) idempotency lookup cost: consider keying the lookup by a deterministic artifact title prefix match using a targeted tracker directory scan (e.g., scan artifact ticket dirs whose alias contains the change-id) or accept and document the bounded cost with a note that `reduce_all_tickets` uses a dir-hash cache (verify the cache is effective here), rather than doing an uncached full scan.
findings.finding: The idempotency lookup (`rebar.list_tickets(ticket_type='code_review')` scanning all tickets for a title match) performs a full `reduce_all_tickets` scan on every review invocation. As the code_review corpus grows (it is explicitly never pruned per AC5), this lookup becomes increasingly expensive and will dominate review latency in production.
findings.location: Section C — Idempotency paragraph; Implementation precision — 'Idempotency lookup uses rebar.list_tickets(ticket_type="code_review")'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` requires any signature change to pass `change_fingerprint`/`norm_id` to the sidecar; if yes, add the `gate_dispatch` module file to the Scope list and add a covering sub-point in AC #3.
findings.finding: Section C references `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(...)` as a call made from `voter.py`, implying this function is either already compatible with `target_ticket` or must be augmented; the Scope/files list does not include the `gate_dispatch` module. If any augmentation to `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` is required (e.g. accepting `change_fingerprint` or `norm_id` kwargs), that file is unscoped and will be missed.
findings.location: Section C ('reuse the existing `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)`') vs. Scope section (lists `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` and `review_bot/voter.py` but not `gate_dispatch`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add a lightweight advisory lock (e.g., a `fcntl` lockfile or `git worktree`-level lock) guarding all worktree writes, OR (b) enforce the single-worker invariant structurally (e.g., a threading.Lock / asyncio.Lock held for the full create→push cycle) and add a unit/integration test that asserts concurrent emit calls serialize correctly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the reviewbot's 'single background worker serializes reviews, so the shared worktree needs no extra lock' without any probe, benchmark, or explicit architectural invariant enforced in code. If this assumption is ever violated (e.g., by a future worker-pool change, a retry concurrent with a live review, or a process restart mid-write), the shared git worktree will corrupt silently — there is no lock, no detection, and no recovery path.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — 'Concurrency: the reviewbot's single background worker serializes reviews, so the shared worktree needs no extra lock.'
findings.suggested_fix: In `voter.py`, wrap the create→push sequence in a module-level `threading.Lock` (since the worker is a background thread). Document this lock in a comment referencing the single-worker assumption so future maintainers know to revisit if the concurrency model changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit a short ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting: (1) the `_NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES` invariant and the exhaustive list of guard sites every member must appear at, (2) the rationale for a first-class type over sidecar, and (3) the `change_fingerprint` / `norm_id` join-key contracts — so future artifact types have a checklist to follow.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel, cross-cutting architectural pattern — a new 'bulk artifact' ticket type with system-wide exemptions across 15+ files, a new join-key contract (`change_fingerprint` / `norm_id` reuse), and a new emit pipeline stage in the reviewbot — but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc recording the decision, its rationale, and the invariants future maintainers must preserve.
findings.location: Section A (entire) — 'mirror session_log at EVERY guard site'; Scope / files list — 15+ files touched.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct Section C step 2's API description: `produce_code_review_verdict` has no `target_ticket` parameter — split into two explicit steps: (a) `verdict = gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(cfg, diff_text=..., ...)`, then (b) `sidecar.emit(verdict, target_ticket=<artifact_id>, repo_root=repo_root)`.
findings.finding: The plan describes `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)` as a single call, implying `target_ticket` is a parameter of `produce_code_review_verdict`. In the actual codebase (adapter.py:187, shim.py:140), `produce_code_review_verdict` accepts no `target_ticket` argument — the sidecar emit is a SEPARATE call: `sidecar.emit(verdict, target_ticket=artifact_id)`. Implementing the plan as written would cause a TypeError at runtime.
findings.location: Section C, step 2: `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)` → `code_review.sidecar.emit`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace: 'reuse the existing gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>) → code_review.sidecar.emit'
With: 'call `verdict = gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(cfg, diff_text=diff_text, commit_message=commit_message, repo_root=repo_root, enabled=True)` then separately call `sidecar.emit(verdict, target_ticket=artifact_id, repo_root=repo_root)`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow the plan-review exemption list to the ONE concrete short-circuit site: `workflow_ops.py:79` (`if pctx.ticket_type in ("bug", "session_log"):`) — remove the four other files (`orchestrator.py`, `__init__.py`, `det_floor.py`, `registry.py`) from this bullet, or annotate each with what (if anything) actually needs changing there.
findings.finding: The plan lists five plan-review files as containing the `('bug','session_log')` short-circuit that needs `code_review` added (`orchestrator.py`, `workflow_ops.py`, `__init__.py`, `det_floor.py`, `registry.py`), but code-search confirms the short-circuit is present in ONLY ONE of them (`workflow_ops.py:79`). The other four files have no `session_log` or `ticket_type` gate. An executor following the plan will search for (and potentially introduce) phantom exemption code in files that don't need it, or miss adding logic to the correct spot.
findings.location: Section A — 'plan-review exemption axis' + `llm/plan_review/{orchestrator.py,workflow_ops.py,__init__.py,det_floor.py,registry.py}`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the five-file list with: '`llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:79` — the single `if pctx.ticket_type in ("bug", "session_log")` guard (add `"code_review"`). No changes needed to `orchestrator.py`, `__init__.py`, `det_floor.py`, or `registry.py` for this exemption.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the idempotency/visibility interaction: the `reads.py:373` inline boolean must also be updated so that `ticket_type='code_review'` passes `exclude_session_logs=False` (using the same `_NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES` set), ensuring `list_tickets(ticket_type='code_review')` returns existing artifacts for the deduplication check.
findings.finding: The plan's `reducer/_api.py` broadening (`exclude_session_logs=True` drops tickets with `ticket_type not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES`) silently breaks the idempotency lookup. The idempotency check calls `rebar.list_tickets(ticket_type='code_review')`, which reaches `reads.py:373` and passes `exclude_session_logs=('code_review' != 'session_log') = True` to `reduce_all_tickets`. After the plan's own change, `reduce_all_tickets` with `exclude_session_logs=True` will DROP all `code_review` tickets (since `'code_review' in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES`). The lookup therefore always returns empty, always creates a fresh artifact, and idempotency per `(change-id, revision)` is silently defeated.
findings.location: Section C — idempotency paragraph + 'Implementation precision' — `reads.py` predicate; also `reducer/_api.py:241-244`
findings.suggested_fix: Update `reads.py:373` from `exclude_session_logs=(ticket_type != "session_log")` to `exclude_session_logs=(ticket_type not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES)`, exactly as the 'Implementation precision' section states — but add this to the AC or Section A guard-site list so the executor does not miss it.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
